nativeRange.habitat: Rocky rapids and stream-like stretches of the upper Rio Negro basin in Amazonas, Brazil, and the Casiquiare drainage linking the Negro to the Orinoco in Venezuela; shelters in crevices and caves among boulders in well-oxygenated, flowing water over sand and rock substrate
nativeRange.biotope: Upper Rio Negro / Casiquiare blackwater rocky rapids

decorPreferences: numerous tight-fitting caves (ceramic, clay tubes, or stacked slate) — at least one cave per male
smooth rockwork and boulders forming crevices
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
driftwood for additional hiding spots and tannin release
strong oxygenation and high water flow (powerheads, river-tank manifold)
subdued lighting or shaded areas to encourage daytime activity

diet.notes: Generally considered an omnivore that leans carnivorous — has been reported to prefer aquatic invertebrates and meaty foods over algae. Sinking carnivore pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, mosquito larvae, Daphnia, Mysis shrimp, and chopped prawn are widely advised as core menu items. Vegetable matter (blanched zucchini, cucumber, algae wafers) is generally accepted as a supplement but should not be the staple. Feed primarily after lights-out; this species tends to be reclusive during the day.

goodWith: Apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids that tolerate warm water
larger peaceful shrimp (Atyopsis, Atya) that won't be eaten
discus (sharing similar temperature and water-chemistry preferences)
avoidWith: multiple adult males in a small tank (intraspecific aggression over caves)
boisterous fast-feeding species that outcompete plecos at feeding time
any fish large enough to harass or predate on plecos

summary: The Snowball Pleco (Hypancistrus inspector) is a black-bodied catfish from the upper Rio Negro and Casiquiare drainage, patterned with bold white spots. It favors warm, well-oxygenated, slightly acidic water with plenty of caves and, unlike many plecos, feeds primarily on meaty foods rather than algae.
careNotes:
The Snowball Pleco is generally regarded as one of the more approachable Hypancistrus for intermediate aquarists, though it shares the genus's preference for warm (around 26-29°C), well-oxygenated, soft, slightly acidic water. High flow is strongly advised — the species occurs naturally in rocky rapids, and stagnant or sluggish water tends to suppress activity and appetite. Powerheads or a river-tank manifold supplementing standard filtration are commonly recommended.

Like its congeners (notably the Zebra Pleco), Hypancistrus inspector is a poor algae grazer and should not be expected to clean glass. Its diet is best treated as carnivore-leaning: sinking high-protein pellets, frozen bloodworms, Mysis, Daphnia, and chopped prawn form the practical staple, with vegetable matter as a supplement. Feeding after lights-out is generally advised, as the species tends to be reclusive during the day.

Caves are essential. Provide at least one snug-fitting cave per male — the fish should be able to barely turn around inside. Multiple Snowball Plecos can be housed together provided each has its own cave and the tank is large enough; conflict between adult males is reported when caves are limited. Importantly, do not house with other Hypancistrus species: hybridisation in captivity is documented and dilutes the species pool.

The Snowball Pleco is widely traded under the L102 designation; the L142 code is sometimes applied in the trade as well, though both are generally treated as the same species. "Inspector Pleco" is occasionally used as a trade name.
breedingNotes: Has been bred successfully in aquaria. Like other Hypancistrus, the Snowball Pleco is a cave-spawner with paternal egg care. A receptive female enters the male's chosen cave and deposits a small clutch (commonly reported around 20-30 eggs); the male fans and guards the clutch for roughly 5-7 days until hatching. Conditioning with varied high-protein live and frozen foods, slightly cooler partial water changes simulating a seasonal flood pulse, and breeding at the upper end of the recommended temperature range (around 28-30°C) are commonly cited triggers. Raise fry on freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worm, and finely crushed high-protein pellets, maintaining the same warm, high-flow, well-oxygenated conditions as the adult tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hypancistrus inspector
primary.notes: Primary source. Covers taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes; Armbruster, 2002), L-number (L102), adult size 14-16 cm, temperature 22-30°C, pH 5.0-7.5, hardness 18-268 ppm, native range (upper Rio Negro and Casiquiare drainage, Brazil/Venezuela), diet (omnivorous, invertebrate-leaning), habitat (rocky rapids), and breeding (cave-spawner with paternal care). PlanetCatfish was unreachable (HTTP 403) on this run, so SeriouslyFish was promoted to primary.

additional.name: FishLaboratory — Snowball Pleco (Hypancistrus inspector, L102)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (14-16 cm / 5.5-6.3 in), temperature 22-30°C, pH 5.0-7.6, hardness 6-10 dKH, lifespan 8-10 years, diet (omnivore, carnivore-leaning), and tankmate guidance. Confirmed solitary disposition and risk of intraspecific male aggression. Tank minimum 40 gal (~151 L) cited; merged with SeriouslyFish 243 L recommendation by biasing toward the larger value and rounding to 200 L.

additional.name: Fishkeeper — Snowball Plec L102 (Hypancistrus inspector)
additional.notes: UK trade source. Confirmed temperature 22-30°C, pH 5.5-7.2, hardness up to 15 dKH, carnivore-leaning diet, bottom-dwelling habit, cave-spawning with male brood care (20-30 eggs, 5-7 day incubation). Stated adult size 18.5 cm — somewhat higher than the 14-16 cm range cited by SeriouslyFish and FishLaboratory; the 14-16 cm consensus was retained as the more frequently reported figure.

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping — Snowball Pleco (L142) Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Notable for documenting the L142 trade designation occasionally used for this species (most authorities use L102). Confirmed warm soft acidic water preference (pH 5.5-7.0, gH 2-10, 26-30°C) and need for high oxygenation and caves.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner with paternal care; small clutches (~20-30 eggs) guarded by male for 5-7 days. Conditioning with high-protein live/frozen foods and cooler water changes is the commonly cited trigger. Generally considered more approachable to breed than H. zebra, though it still benefits from a dedicated breeding setup with warm, high-flow water and species-appropriate caves.
